title: Fitting engineered wood flooring, glued method
subtitle: Bond engineered boards directly to a solid subfloor for a stable, permanent finish
excerpt: How to glue engineered wood flooring to a concrete or existing tile subfloor. Covers subfloor preparation, adhesive spreading, board direction, and the expansion-gap rule that makes or breaks a glued floor.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. BS 8203:2017 Code of practice for installation of resilient floor coverings.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clear perimeter space, typically 10 to 12 mm, left between the flooring and every wall or fixed object. Wood moves with humidity; without the gap, buckling is certain.
A flooring board with a real hardwood veneer bonded to a plywood or HDF core. More dimensionally stable than solid timber, suitable for gluing to concrete.
A measurement of the residual moisture content in a concrete subfloor before laying. Concrete must read below 75% relative humidity (or 2.5% moisture content by mass) before adhesive goes down.

content.text: Gluing
engineered wood boards

directly to a solid subfloor holds them hard against the base. This removes the movement you get with floating floors and makes it the right choice for concrete floors and underfloor heating. The job divides into two parts: subfloor preparation, which takes most of the time, and laying, which goes quickly once the prep is done.
Step 1, Check the subfloor moisture
Concrete continues to release moisture for months after pouring, and many older slabs are still damp enough to destroy an adhesive bond. Carry out a
subfloor moisture test

using a calibrated hygrometer or a calcium chloride kit. If the reading is above 75% relative humidity, fit a surface damp-proof membrane before the adhesive, typically a two-part epoxy DPM, applied at 300 to 500 g/m² in two coats 24 hours apart. Do not proceed without it.
Step 2, Flatten the subfloor

Check for high spots with a 1200 mm spirit level. Any undulation greater than 3 mm over a 1800 mm span needs filling before the boards go down. Sand down mortar blobs and ridges; fill hollows with floor-levelling compound (self-levelling or trowel-grade). Wait for the compound to reach full cure before testing the moisture again, most compounds add their own moisture load.
Step 3, Acclimatise the boards

Leave the unopened boxes flat in the room where they'll be laid for at least 3 days (48 hours for rooms with stable central heating; 5 days in summer). The boards take on the room's humidity and expand or contract before they're glued. Stacking on the subfloor rather than standing against a wall is important, vertical boards rest on one edge and bow.
Step 4, Plan the layout

Run the boards parallel to the longest wall or parallel to the main light source, both work; pick whichever looks better in your room. Measure the width of the room perpendicular to the run direction. Divide by the board width to find the number of rows and the width of the cut row at the far wall. If the cut row would be less than 50 mm, increase the first row by half a board width to make both edge rows roughly equal. Mark the starting line with a chalk line or a straightedge.
Step 5, Apply the adhesive and lay the first boards

Use a flexible flooring adhesive rated for engineered wood (SBR-based or MS-polymer). Spread with the notched trowel specified by the adhesive manufacturer, typically a 6 mm × 6 mm V-notch for boards up to 15 mm thick. Work in manageable bays, no more than 1 m wide. Comb the adhesive at a consistent 45° angle, avoiding ridges. Place the first board with its tongue side facing the starting wall, leaving a 10 to 12 mm
expansion gap

held with timber spacers. Press the board firmly into the adhesive with a light blow from the rubber mallet through a tapping block (a spare off-cut works). Check the surface with the spirit level at every third board.
Step 6, Continue the field and cut the edge rows

Stagger end joints by at least 300 mm between adjacent rows. For cut boards, mark the cut line on the face side and cut with a jigsaw or tenon saw. Cope around door frames with a coping saw or jigsaw. The final row against the far wall needs cutting rip-wise; mark the cut line with a marking gauge or a compass running along the wall, then rip with the jigsaw. Feed the board into the gap, tapping the cut edge gently to close the long joint.
Step 7, Finish and remove spacers

Leave the adhesive to cure for the manufacturer's specified time before removing the expansion spacers, typically 24 hours for MS-polymer adhesive. Do not allow foot traffic on the floor during the cure. Once spacers are out, fit timber or rubber-backed beading over the expansion gap at every wall, fixing the beading to the wall (never to the floor). Fit door threshold strips where the engineered floor meets carpet, tile, or another floor covering.
Variations

Over underfloor heating: use a flexible adhesive rated for UFH and keep the system at working temperature for 3 days before and during laying. Rapid temperature swings after laying are the main cause of joint opening.

Over existing floor tiles: check that the tiles are well bonded and that the additional floor height will not jam the door. Grind down any proud grout lines and apply a self-levelling compound if the tile surface undulates more than 3 mm.
Herringbone or chevron layout: adhesive quantities increase because every board needs more coverage at the angled ends. Allow 20% more adhesive and 30% more boards for cut waste.
Care

Wipe spills immediately. Hardwood veneers will not tolerate standing water or sustained damp. Clean with a slightly damp mop and a pH-neutral wood-floor cleaner. Avoid steam mops. Sand and re-oil or re-lacquer the floor when the surface starts to look dull, typically every 5 to 10 years depending on traffic.
Troubleshooter

items.symptom: Board edges lifting away from the subfloor
items.cause: Adhesive applied too thickly or too thinly, so the notched trowel ridges didn't collapse under the board weight
items.fix: Press down firmly and weight with books or sandbags during cure. For persistent lifts, inject a small amount of wood-floor adhesive under the board edge with a syringe and clamp overnight.

items.symptom: Boards buckling or joints opening after a few weeks
items.cause: Expansion gap too small, or boards not fully acclimatised before laying
items.fix: Remove the wall beading and check whether the gap has closed. If so, trim the board edges with a router or oscillating multi-tool to restore the gap. Ensure the room temperature and humidity are stable.

items.cause: Air voids under the boards where the adhesive did not transfer from the subfloor
items.fix: Drill a 3 mm hole through the board centre in the hollow area (in an inconspicuous spot or under furniture) and inject flooring adhesive. Weight the area for 24 hours.
